summary: NestJS library ships hardcoded API-key fallback and generic HTTP-proxy endpoint
details: common-tg-service is a NestJS library whose AuthGuard accepts the literal string 'santoor' as a valid x-api-key whenever the consumer has not set process.env.X_API_KEY (dist/guards/auth.guard.js around line 88). The same fallback is used as the outgoing 'x-api-key' header on requests the library makes to author-controlled endpoints (cms.paidgirls.site/ip-management, helper-thge.onrender.com/execute-request) and on the proxy path it exposes. The library also ships an AppController route POST /execute-request that proxies an arbitrary caller-supplied URL/method/headers/body via axios and injects that same 'santoor' fallback header. A consumer who wires AppModule into their NestJS app without overriding X_API_KEY therefore exposes a generic outbound HTTP proxy authenticated by a publicly known string, plus an IP/origin allowlist that includes the author's own infrastructure (paidgirls.site, zomcall.netlify.app, etc.). There are no install or import-time side effects — no lifecycle scripts, no top-level network calls, no credential or filesystem scraping; the risky surface only materialises when the consumer explicitly registers AppModule and runs the resulting NestJS app. The hardcoded fallback and bundled SSRF-shaped proxy are quality and design weaknesses that warrant human review before downstream use, but they do not by themselves exfiltrate installer data or execute attacker-controlled code on the installer's machine.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
